Tabstack by Mozilla

Tabstack by Mozilla [...] Atta 🐬
StartupBase

StartupBase

Apache 2.0 on both checkpoints is the real unlock here. Most frontier-level models stay closed exactly at the point where they become useful for production workloads.

Curious what fine-tuning looks like for teams that want to specialize it on a specific codebase or domain. Is that straightforward with the current weights?

FOREST TOP

Long-horizon coding needs evaluation beyond task completion. How do you measure recovery after bad edits, context drift across long runs, unnecessary tool calls, and whether a human can reconstruct why the agent made each decision?

Winsten Rasyada

The open weights are what caught my attention. Being able to run and tune a serious coding model on your own infrastructure is a big plus. [...] Anand Thakkar

The 256K context window at 23B active params is a strong architectural bet. Long-horizon agentic tasks without chunking is where most models fall apart. We've been navigating the inference infra tradeoff for our own agent layer, and self-hosted open weights changes the calculus significantly. How does Laguna handle attention at max context? Any sparse attention or positional tricks that keep it tractable?

Sanjay Kumar

Open weights with a 256K context window at 23B active params is a big deal for agentic coding, that should really help on long-horizon refactors where context runs out fast. Curious how Laguna M.1 holds up inside Cursor or Claude Code style loops. Congrats on shipping.

Atta 🐬

results.title: Deep Work Plan: Models matter. Context matters ... - Product Hunt
results.content:
which, as you said, is most of the battle.

Dogan Akbulut

The "repo as harness" idea is clever — giving agents durable context instead of a fresh chat window every time is exactly what long-horizon tasks need. Context drift is probably the #1 reason agent work falls apart mid-task.

Is the plan file something you generate once and manually update, or does it evolve automatically as the codebase changes?

Sergio Florez

Viktor.com

What do you think? … [...] The part I'd really stress: keeping things current is work the plan does, not a separate chore. Any task that changes behavior also updates the `docs/`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.agents/` kit that describe it, and extends the tests that prove it, and that re-sync is part of the task's own validation gate. On top of that, every plan closes with a security-analysis pass and a skill-discovery step that turns what was just built into reusable skills. So the docs and tests evolve alongside the code by construction. The agent is continuously self-documenting, instead of leaving a stale spec behind.

It's basically the Boy Scout rule applied to the harness: every run leaves the repo a little more agent-ready than it found it, not more stale.

Longer take in the methodology write-up:

Across the most-reviewed foundation models, the market splits between broad API platforms, open and portable stacks, and specialist reasoning or multimodal options. OpenAI stands out for end-to-end app building with tools, memory, voice, vision, and agents; Mistral AI emphasizes privacy, self-hosting, and multilingual enterprise workflows; DeepSeek competes on affordable coding, research, and long-document analysis."
